meta.title: Welcome to Bridge
meta.description: Course overview and what bridge is

blocks.content:
## Welcome to Contract Bridge

Contract bridge is widely considered the greatest card game ever invented. It has captivated presidents, scientists, billionaires, and casual players alike for nearly a century. Warren Buffett plays it. Bill Gates plays it. Alan Turing analyzed it. And once you learn it, you'll understand why.

Bridge is a game of **partnership, communication, and deduction**. Unlike most card games, you don't play alone — you work with a partner sitting across from you at the table, and together you try to outperform the opposing partnership. The catch? You can only communicate through the bids you make and the cards you play. No winks, no signals, no whispering. The game itself is your only language.

## The Big Picture

Here's what a bridge game looks like from 30,000 feet:

- **Four players** sit at a table, forming **two partnerships**: North-South vs. East-West.
- A standard **52-card deck** is dealt, giving each player **13 cards**.
- The players conduct an **auction** (also called bidding) to determine what the "contract" will be — essentially, a promise by one partnership to win a certain number of tricks.
- One player becomes the **declarer** (the one who plays the hand), their partner becomes the **dummy** (whose cards are placed face-up on the table), and the other two become **defenders**.
- **13 tricks** are played. Each trick consists of four cards, one from each player.
- At the end, you compare how many tricks the declaring side won against what they promised in the contract.

That's it. Deal, bid, play, score. But within that simple framework lies extraordinary depth.

## Why Bridge Is Special

**It rewards thinking, not luck.** Yes, the cards are dealt randomly, but over time, skill dominates. The best bridge players in the world win consistently — not because they get better cards, but because they make better decisions with the cards they're dealt.

**It's a partnership game.** You succeed or fail together. Learning to communicate effectively with your partner — through bids and card play — is one of the most rewarding aspects of the game.

**It's endlessly deep.** You can learn the basics in a few hours, but mastery takes a lifetime. Every hand presents a new puzzle, and even world champions encounter situations they've never seen before.

**It builds real skills.** Bridge develops logical reasoning, probability assessment, memory, and the ability to make decisions with incomplete information. It's no coincidence that many top bridge players excel in fields like mathematics, law, and finance.

## What You'll Learn in This Course

This course takes you from complete beginner to competent player across ten levels:

| Level | Title | What You'll Learn |
|-------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | **The Table** | How the game works — deals, tricks, auctions, scoring |
| 2 | **The Hand** | Evaluating what your 13 cards are worth |
| 3 | **The Opening** | How to start the bidding conversation |
| 4 | **The Response** | How to answer your partner's opening bid |
| 5 | **The Rebid** | Completing the bidding dialogue |
| 6 | **The Play** | Declarer techniques to win tricks |
| 7 | **The Defense** | How to beat the other side's contract |
| 8 | **The Conventions** | Special bidding agreements that expand your vocabulary |
| 9 | **The Battle** | Competitive bidding when both sides want the contract |
| 10 | **The Expert** | Advanced strategy for serious players |

## Let's Get Started

In this first level, we'll walk through everything that happens at the bridge table — from the moment the cards are dealt to the final score. By the end of Level 1, you'll understand the complete flow of a bridge deal and be ready to start evaluating your hand.

No prior card game experience is needed. If you've never played any card game at all, you'll be just fine. And if you've played other trick-taking games like Spades, Hearts, or Euchre, you'll find some familiar concepts here — but bridge adds layers that make it uniquely rewarding.

Ready? Let's sit down at the table.
